---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "<$",
  fig.path = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

# rDataSet

<!-- badges: start -->
<!-- badges: end -->

rDataSet is introduces a new S3 style dataset object. A dataset is nothing else but a tibble with id columns. Where the id columns like in a relational database must uniqely define each row. But instead of following the path of relational databases. From here on the focus is on dataset merging. Merging in this context means we have two datasets with some overlapping domain and we want to get a single dataset as an output. When merging we have three disticnt parts. The rows only present in the left side, the intersection, and the rows only present on the right side. For each we can decide wether we want to keep them in in the result. Further we have to decide for the intersection wether we take the values from left, from right or apply some other function to merge the two (maybe disallow this function thing as we never know what type of data we need to merge, left right is the only safe function?).

i have the fear that this actually just could be a join with some merge function. and that all the other blabber is for nothing. well i still think that masking is a nice feature. and dropping ids seems realy cool. but we might actually reduce the whole thing to a ids_by functions similar to group by?
well i still have some nice features which i want to cover. maybe this fear hints at the possibilty to include the ids into the function like, mask_with(a,b,ids=...) which would make the step of creating the datasets obsolete in the first place.

- writing datasets as readable text files. (maybe its own project -> its own project)
- having piped data control e.g. through hashing (identity), or less restrictiv messures, e.g. through a mask with catch all behaviour. (certainly its own project)
so what are the goals until we say this is complete. I think we have discoverd some fancy transformations the long, wide and decomposed data formats. I'm afraid that these are too low level to every be used in a daily case. But we can build some usefull functions on top of them like, mask_with, and select_ids(). It might also be more than helpfull for text representations. 

In application this might look like this:
```R
# our original dataset containing our animals and some mistakes
dataset <- tibble(id = 1:4, id2 = c(1,1,2,2) , vals = c("cow", "horse", "car", NA), vals2 = c("animal", "animal", "other", "other")) %>%
  dataset_build(ids = c("id", "id2"))

# in fact we have to cats. instead of recoding directly in the data we create a patch
patch <- tibble(id2 = 2:3, vals = c("cat", "dog")) %>%
  dataset_build(ids = c("id2"))

# and mask the wrong data with the patch
dataset %>%
  mask_with(patch)

# another fancy thing is to drop id columns
# only value columns which are functionaly dependent on the
# selected id cols will remain in the dataset.
dataset %>%
  select_ids(-id) # tidy select on ids

dataset %>%
  dataset_flatten()
```

```R
# we can merge two datasets with a set operation and specifing a precedence
# empty values are dropped by default.
dataset %>%
  merge_with(patch, set_op = "setdiff", prec = "left")

dataset %>%
  merge_with(patch, set_op = "left", prec = "right")

dataset %>%
  merge_with(patch, set_op = "intersect", prec = "left", keep = TRUE)

dataset %>%
  merge_with(patch, set_op = "union", prec = "left")
```

## Installation

You can install the development version of rDataSet from local source like so:

``` r
devtools::install("/path/to/rDataSet")
# or with
install.packages("/path/to/rDataSet", repo = NULL, type = "source")
```

## Set Operations

Set Operations look real cool but I'm pivoting away from using operators like `+` as documentation of these functions is hard and it is impossible to set any flags. Similar goals can be achieved with pipe style opperations like `|> mask_with()` which is more readable allows for altering behaviour through flags and can be documented. All functionalities from below are captured in `merge_with` with the apropriate flags.

- `+` `merge_with(a,b, set_op = "union", "left")`
- `-` `merge_with(a,b, set_op = "setdiff", "left")`
- `>` `merge_with(a,b, set_op = "intersect", "left")`

Each set operation works in two stages:

1. **Row/Column Alignment**: Determines which rows and columns appear in the result
2. **Value Operation**: Decides which values to keep for each cell

| Operation | Rows in Result | Columns in Result | Value Operation |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Difference (`A - B`) | All rows from A | All value columns from A | Keep values from A where B has `NA` |
| Intersection (`A > B`) | Only rows present in **both** A and B (inner join on IDs) | Only common value columns | Keep values from A where B has non-`NA` |
| Union (`A + B`) | All rows from both A and B (full join on IDs) | All value columns from both | Use A's value; fall back to B where A has `NA` |
| Equality (`A == B`) | Must match exactly (same rows) | Must match exactly (same columns) | Must match exactly (same value) |

**Note**: ID columns must match between datasets for all operations. Value columns that exist in only one dataset are handled according to the join type (dropped, kept, or error).

## License

GPL (>= 3)
